title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who participated in environmental volunteering. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Green Hands: Volunteering for the Planet

texts.A:
### Oscar, Urban Reforestation
'I wanted to see more green in my own city,' Oscar explains. [51]The most difficult part was convincing local residents that planting trees wouldn't block their light or decrease property values.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that digging in the dirt was far more exhausting than my gym sessions.[/48] [55]I’d initially planned to stay for a month, but seeing the first saplings thrive made me extend my commitment indefinitely.[/55] [50]I realized that my management background was vital for organizing the volunteer shifts and securing tool donations.[/50] [53]It has fundamentally changed how I view my neighborhood; I now feel a deep sense of ownership and responsibility for every leaf.[/53] It’s deeply rewarding work.
texts.B:
### Maya, Beach Cleanups
Maya organizes coastal cleanups. 'The amount of microplastic is staggering,' she says. [47]I hadn't expected to feel such a strong sense of anger every time I saw a littered beach, which was a new emotion for me.[/47] [54]What kept me going was the unexpected bond I formed with a group of elderly retirees who never missed a session.[/54] [52]I initially worried that the task was too small to make a real difference, but seeing the local bird population return was proof enough.[/52] [49]The experience taught me that collective action is the only antidote to the feeling of climate despair.[/49] [56]If I hadn't attended a public lecture on ocean health, I’d still be oblivious to the scale of the crisis.[/56] It’s a labor of love.
texts.C:
### Leo, Wildlife Corridor Building
Leo worked on a project to connect fragmented habitats. 'We were building bridges for squirrels and badgers,' he laughs. [50]I found that my hobby of birdwatching helped me identify which areas needed the most protection.[/50] [47]The sheer amount of physical labor involved was a shock, as I’d spent my whole life at a desk.[/47] [53]I now understand that conservation is as much about politics and land rights as it is about animals.[/53] [51]I struggled with the slow pace of bureaucratic approval, which felt like it was hindering our urgent work.[/51] [54]However, the camaraderie among the diverse group of volunteers made the rainy days bearable.[/54] [52]I’d originally thought the project was just about the environment, but it turned out to be about community building too.
texts.D:
### Sophie, Community Gardening
Sophie transformed a vacant lot into a vegetable garden. 'I had no idea how to grow anything,' she admits. [48]I was amazed at how much my physical health improved simply by spending four hours a week outdoors.[/48] [49]The project helped me realize that our food system is incredibly fragile and that local resilience is key.[/49] [56]If the local council hadn't provided a small grant for seeds, we never would have gotten off the ground.[/56] [52]I’d worried that people wouldn't respect the space, but there has been zero vandalism since we started.[/52] [55]It took a full year of trial and error before we produced enough to share with the local food bank.[/55] The garden has become the heart of our street.

questions.question: mentions experiencing a specific and unfamiliar emotional reaction?
questions.explanation: Maya (B) felt a 'strong sense of anger' which was a 'new emotion', and Leo (C) was 'shocked' by the labor.

questions.question: notes that the physical nature of the work exceeded their expectations?
questions.explanation: Oscar (A) found it more exhausting than the gym, and Sophie (D) mentions health improvement from the work.

questions.question: expresses a shift in their understanding of a global or systemic issue?
questions.explanation: Maya (B) learned about collective action as an antidote to despair, and Sophie (D) realized the fragility of the food system.

questions.question: mentions that a previous interest or professional skill was unexpectedly helpful?
questions.explanation: Oscar (A) used his management background, and Leo (C) used his birdwatching hobby.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to people's attitudes or official processes?
questions.explanation: Oscar (A) had to convince residents, and Leo (C) struggled with bureaucratic approval.

questions.question: admits to having had doubts about the impact or success of the project?
questions.explanation: Maya (B) worried the task was too small, and Sophie (D) worried about potential vandalism.

questions.question: mentions a change in how they perceive their immediate surroundings or a field of study?
questions.explanation: Oscar (A) views his neighborhood with a sense of ownership, and Leo (C) understands the political side of conservation.

questions.question: highlights the value of the social connections made during the project?
questions.explanation: Maya (B) mentions the bond with retirees, and Leo (C) mentions the camaraderie making rainy days bearable.

questions.question: mentions that the results of their efforts took a significant amount of time to appear?
questions.explanation: Sophie (D) says it took a full year before they could share food, and Oscar (A) extended his stay after seeing saplings thrive.

questions.question: acknowledges that an external factor was crucial for their involvement or the project’s start?
questions.explanation: Maya (B) credits a public lecture, and Sophie (D) credits a council grant.
